name: locators
context: Choose resilient Playwright locators using roles, labels, text, test IDs, filters, frames, and auto-waiting actionability rules.
info: Locators are Playwright's core abstraction for finding elements and waiting until they are actionable. This context teaches the selector priority agents should use: user-facing role and accessible-name locators first, then labels and text, then test IDs, with CSS/XPath as escape hatches. It also covers chained locators, filtering, strictness, frames, shadow DOM, auto-waiting, and why arbitrary sleeps create flaky automation. Agents use it whenever writing tests, scripts, or translating CLI exploration into durable Playwright code.

sections.title: Locator priority order
sections.body: Use `getByRole(role, { name })` first because it tests the same accessible contract users and assistive technologies rely on. Use `getByLabel()` for form fields, `getByPlaceholder()` when labels are unavailable, and `getByText()` for visible copy. Use `getByTestId()` for intentionally stable app contracts. CSS and XPath are fallback tools for technical DOM details, not the default.

sections.title: Strictness and filtering
sections.body: Actions require a locator to resolve to one element. If a locator matches many elements, refine it with accessible name, `filter({ hasText })`, `filter({ has })`, `first()`, `last()`, or `nth(index)`. Prefer semantic refinement over positional selection; `nth()` is acceptable for ordered lists only when order is part of the contract.

sections.title: Actionability
sections.body: Playwright auto-waits for actionability before user actions. A click waits for visibility, stability, receiving events, and enabled state. A fill waits for an editable input. Use this instead of `waitForTimeout`. If a race remains, wait for the real condition: a URL, a response, a locator state, an event, or a web-first assertion.

sections.title: Frames and shadow DOM
sections.body: Use `frameLocator()` for iframes: `page.frameLocator('iframe[name=checkout]').getByRole('button', { name: 'Pay' })`. Playwright locators pierce open shadow roots by default for most selector types. Closed shadow roots are not inspectable; rely on public UI or app-provided test hooks.
